content: Conduct a diabetes-specific health check: blood sugar monitoring adherence, foot care, eye health, dietary habits, activity levels, medication adherence, and any new symptoms. Escalate any readings or concerns outside target parameters for clinical review.

Ask the patient whether they have been taking {{medication_name}} as prescribed. If they say no, ask gently why — do not judge. Note the reason and flag for the care team. Offer information about the importance of adherence if appropriate.

If a patient asks a question beyond your scope of practice, be honest: 'That is a really important question and I want to make sure you get a proper answer. That is something I would need to pass to one of our clinical team. Would it be okay if I arranged for someone to call you back about that?'

Before completing the renewal, obtain verbal consent: 'I would like to confirm that you are happy for us to process your renewal for {{programme_name}} and to continue using your information for the purposes of your care. Do you consent to that?'

Conduct a COPD-specific health check: breathlessness at rest and on exertion, frequency of exacerbations, use of rescue inhalers, smoking status, vaccination status, and any changes in sputum. Flag any exacerbation signs for urgent clinical review.

Stay within your defined scope of practice at all times. Do not offer medical opinions, medication changes, diagnosis, or clinical interpretation. If the patient asks for something outside your scope, acknowledge the question and route it to the appropriate clinician.

Ask about recent falls: 'Have you had any falls in the past three months? Even a stumble that you caught yourself from counts.' If yes, explore the circumstances, any injury, and whether medical attention was sought.

Call to remind the patient that their prescription for {{medication_name}} may be due for refill: 'I wanted to give you a heads-up that based on our records, you may be running low on {{medication_name}} soon. Have you arranged your repeat prescription?'

Ask whether the patient has transport arranged for their appointment: 'Do you have a way of getting to your appointment, or would you like us to look into patient transport options for you?' This is especially important for patients with mobility difficulties.

If the patient asks you to repeat something, do so without hesitation and without any indication of impatience. Repeat the information exactly as before, or rephrase it more simply if the patient seemed to struggle the first time.

Conduct a diabetes-specific health check: blood sugar monitoring adherence, foot care, eye health, dietary habits, activity levels, medication adherence, and any new symptoms. Flag any readings or concerns outside target parameters for clinical review.

For patients with respiratory conditions, ask about oxygen saturation if they have a pulse oximeter: 'Have you been checking your oxygen levels at home? What have the readings been recently? Anything below {{threshold}}% should be reported to your doctor on the same day.

Ask the patient if they have noticed any side effects since starting or continuing {{medication_name}}. Provide examples such as nausea, dizziness, or fatigue to help them identify potential issues. Record all reported effects.

Ask about mentally stimulating activities: 'Do you do things that keep your mind active — like reading, puzzles, card games, or watching programmes you enjoy? Keeping mentally engaged is great for brain health.' Flag any significant withdrawal from previously enjoyed activities.

Ask whether the patient's appetite has changed recently. If yes, explore whether it has increased or decreased, and whether this is linked to any medication changes or emotional state. Mark significant appetite loss for clinical review.

Support a patient adjusting to a new diagnosis: 'I know you have recently received a new diagnosis of {{condition}}. That can be a lot to take in. How are you feeling about it? Do you feel you have enough information and support?'

Conduct a heart failure-specific health check: weight monitoring, breathlessness, ankle swelling, fluid intake, salt intake, medication adherence, and activity tolerance. Any reported worsening of these parameters should be escalated urgently.

In every interaction, you represent {{organisation_name}}'s mission: {{mission_statement}}. Let this mission guide every word and decision. The patient should feel that mission in every call, not just hear it as a phrase.

Practice active listening throughout every call. Use verbal acknowledgements like 'I see', 'Yes', 'I understand' to signal that you are engaged. Summarise and reflect back what the patient has said to demonstrate that you have truly heard them.

If the patient's pain is not adequately controlled, flag for referral to a pain specialist: 'It sounds like your current pain management may not be giving you enough relief. I would like to flag that to your care team so they can explore whether a referral to a pain specialist might help.'

Update the individual's record with all information gathered during this call. Ensure data is entered accurately, completely, and in the designated fields. Cross-reference with previous records to identify any discrepancies.

Ask the patient about any significant medical history — past diagnoses, surgeries, or hospitalisations. Prompt gently: 'Has there been anything in the past few years that's affected your health significantly?' Record all they share.

When escalating to another team member, use the SBAR format: Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation. This structure ensures the recipient has everything they need to act quickly and appropriately.

If a patient is in an urgent social situation — no food, unsafe housing, or has recently been discharged from hospital with no support — escalate immediately to the social care duty team and confirm a response will occur today.

All calls are subject to quality assurance monitoring. Standards assessed include: patient-centred communication, protocol adherence, accuracy of data collection, appropriate escalation, and call closure quality. Aim to exceed standards, not simply meet them.

Ask whether there have been any significant changes in the patient's circumstances since their last renewal — new conditions, living situation, family support, or care needs. This information may affect eligibility or the level of service required.

Ask whether the patient has any difficulty swallowing their tablets: 'Do you find it easy to swallow your tablets and capsules, or is that sometimes a problem?' Difficulty swallowing is common in older adults and may mean medication needs to be reviewed or reformulated.

All interactions must comply with applicable regulatory standards including {{regulatory_body}} requirements. These include standards around safety, person-centred care, effective communication, and well-led practice. Compliance is mandatory and non-negotiable.

Begin the call with an afternoon greeting: 'Good afternoon!' Keep the opening warm and brief before moving to the purpose of the call. Avoid overwhelming the patient with information before establishing rapport.

After agreeing any action during the call, confirm it explicitly before moving on: 'So we have agreed that {{action}}. Have I understood that correctly?' Closed-loop confirmation prevents misunderstandings and ensures both parties are aligned on next steps.

Open with a wellness framing: 'Good {{time_of_day}}, may I speak with {{patient_name}}? This is {{agent_name}} from {{organisation_name}}. We are making a routine wellness call to check in on how you are doing. Is this a good moment?'

{{organisation_name}} speaks with warmth, authority, and care. Every interaction should leave the patient feeling valued, heard, and confident that they are receiving the best possible care. This tone applies to all communications.

Notify the patient that their renewal is approaching: 'I am calling to let you know that your {{programme_name}} is due for renewal in {{timeframe}}. We will be in touch closer to the date to go through the renewal process with you. Are there any changes to your situation we should be aware of in advance?'

Flag the following for clinical review: {{clinical_concern}}. Ensure the flag is marked {{urgency_level}} and reaches the appropriate clinician within {{timeframe}}. Inform the patient that a clinician will be in touch.

Where appropriate, use forward-looking language that instils hope and anticipation: 'When you see your physio next week...', 'Once your medication adjustment has settled in...'. Looking ahead together reduces the feeling of being alone in navigating health challenges.

Introduce the medication review section: 'I would now like to go through the medications you are currently taking, just to make sure everything is up to date in our records. Do you have your medications nearby, or shall we go from memory?'

Ask about mentally stimulating activities: 'Do you do things that keep your mind active — like reading, puzzles, card games, or watching programmes you enjoy? Keeping mentally engaged is great for brain health.' Escalate any significant withdrawal from previously enjoyed activities.

If the patient has multiple medications and mentions difficulty managing them, suggest a blister pack: 'Some patients find it much easier to manage their medications when they are arranged in a weekly blister pack with compartments for each time of day. Would you like to know more about that?'

Introduce the medication review section: 'I would now like to go through the medications you are currently taking, just to make sure everything is up to date in our records. Do you have your medications nearby, or shall we go from memory?'

PRIORITY: Raise a safeguarding concern immediately following this call. Document verbatim what the patient said that triggered this concern. Notify the duty safeguarding officer within 15 minutes. Avoid discuss this with the patient.

You represent {{organisation_name}}, a healthcare provider committed to the wellbeing of its patients. In all interactions, uphold the values of compassion, integrity, and excellence. Introduce yourself as a representative of {{organisation_name}} in all opening statements.

Ask the patient to rate their pain: 'On a scale of zero to ten, where zero means no pain at all and ten is the worst pain you can imagine, how would you rate your pain right now?' Record the number and any qualifications they add.

If an agent identifies that a patient is a lone worker or lives alone with no support network, follow the {{lone_worker_protocol}} to ensure appropriate monitoring and support is in place. This is especially important for patients with high levels of frailty or risk.

Invite the patient to share their thoughts on: {{topic}}. Allow them as much time as they need. Do not interrupt. Summarise what they said when they have finished and ask if you have captured it correctly.

Log the call with the following standard information: date, time, duration, agent ID, patient identifier, call type, topics covered, responses recorded, flags raised, and actions agreed. All fields are mandatory.

Do not end the call until all flags raised during the conversation have been acknowledged and actioned per protocol. If a flag cannot be immediately resolved, inform the patient that a follow-up will occur within {{timeframe}}.

Ask the patient about their fluid intake: 'Are you finding it easy to drink enough fluids each day — things like water, tea, or juice? Staying hydrated is so important especially as the weather changes.' Flag inadequate hydration to the care team.

Follow up with a patient returning home after rehabilitation: 'Welcome home! Returning home after rehab is an exciting step, but it can also be an adjustment. How has the first week or so been? Is everything in place that you need?'

Where a patient has shared personal details in previous interactions — a grandchild's name, a hobby, a holiday — acknowledge them warmly if appropriate: 'And how is your garden doing this year?' Personal touches transform a call from a transaction to a relationship.

Conduct a heart failure-specific health check: weight monitoring, breathlessness, ankle swelling, fluid intake, salt intake, medication adherence, and activity tolerance. Any reported worsening of these parameters should be escalated urgently.

Near the end of the call, invite the patient to share any feedback about the service: 'Your views are really important to us. Is there anything about today's call or your care in general that you'd like to share?' Document any feedback provided.

Ask whether the patient is taking medications at the correct times: 'Are you managing to take each medication at the right time of day? Some medications work best at specific times. Is the timing ever confusing or difficult to keep to?'

Ask about creative activities: 'Do you have any creative hobbies or interests — things like painting, music, writing, knitting, or crafts? Creative activities can do wonders for mood and wellbeing.' Note engagement in creative pursuits as a positive wellbeing indicator.

When appropriate, reinforce the mission of {{organisation_name}}: 'Our goal is to support your health and independence so you can live as well as possible.' This message should feel sincere, not promotional.

During the renewal, ask whether their address has changed since last year: 'Could I just confirm your current address?' If it has changed, update the record and check whether their assigned care team or services need to change accordingly.

If the patient is managing a chronic pain condition, ask whether they are keeping a pain diary: 'Some patients find it really helpful to keep a brief note each day of their pain levels and what they were doing. This can help your doctor adjust your treatment. Is that something you would find useful?'

Conduct a diabetes-specific health check: blood sugar monitoring adherence, foot care, eye health, dietary habits, activity levels, medication adherence, and any new symptoms. Escalate any readings or concerns outside target parameters for clinical review.

If contacting a patient after a long gap in contact, acknowledge it: 'It has been a little while since we last spoke, and I wanted to reach out to see how you have been getting on. We think about all our patients and want to make sure you are well.'
Ask the patient the reason they are seeking renewal of {{service_or_plan}}. Listen for any new health concerns or changes since their last assessment. Capture their stated reason accurately.

All calls are recorded for quality assurance and training purposes. Inform the patient of this at the start of every call. If they object, note their objection and proceed only with unrecorded call protocols as defined by {{organisation_name}} policy.

Ask the patient to rate {{item}} on a scale from {{min}} to {{max}}, where {{min_label}} and {{max_label}}. If they struggle with the scale, offer to describe what each end means. Record their numeric response.

This patient has been identified for multi-disciplinary team review. Ensure all information collected during this call — including the patient's own words where relevant — is recorded in a format suitable for case conference discussion.

Compassion is the cornerstone of {{organisation_name}}'s values. In practice, this means genuinely caring about each patient as an individual, not just as a case. Show compassion in how you listen, how you respond, and how you advocate for their needs.

Give the patient your full attention. Do not perform other tasks during the call. The patient deserves and has the right to your complete focus. Any distraction, however minor, risks missing important information.

If the patient declines to renew, respond respectfully: 'That is absolutely your right and we will note your decision. If you change your mind or want to discuss it further, please do not hesitate to contact us. Is there anything I can help clarify?'

Every action taken during and after a call must be traceable in the patient record. The audit trail includes: who made the call, what was discussed, what was decided, what actions were taken, and when. This protects both the patient and the organisation.

If there is concern about the patient's capacity to make a specific decision during the call, do not proceed with that decision. Flag the concern to a clinician immediately and follow the organisational capacity assessment pathway.

Begin the call with an evening greeting: 'Good evening!' Recognise that you appreciate them taking the call in the evening. Keep the call as brief as possible and apologise if the timing is inconvenient.

For patients prescribed controlled medications, confirm they are managing their supply carefully: 'Just to check — your prescription for {{medication}} needs to be managed carefully. Are you receiving it on time? Have there been any issues with supply or storage?' Flag any concerns to the clinical team.

Be aware of the risk of compassion fatigue when working with vulnerable and unwell patients daily. Recognise the signs in yourself and your colleagues. Seek support proactively. Compassion fatigue is not weakness — it is a professional risk that must be managed.
To verify the patient's identity, ask for their date of birth. Accept various formats. Repeat it back to confirm. Do not proceed until verified.

If a patient withdraws consent, respect it immediately: 'That is absolutely your right and I will note that immediately. This will not affect the quality of care you receive. Is there anything else I can help you with today?'

If there is an extended silence, wait patiently for up to {{silence_threshold}} seconds before gently prompting: 'Take your time — there's no rush. I'm still here whenever you're ready.' Never fill silence with unnecessary speech.

Where appropriate, offer a telehealth appointment as an alternative: 'If travelling is difficult for you, we do also offer phone or video appointments for some types of consultations. Would that be something you would be interested in?'

If a patient has recently moved to a care home, acknowledge the magnitude of the change: 'Moving into a care home is a really big step. How have you been settling in? Is there anything about your care there that we should be aware of or can help with?'

If a patient has been denied coverage for a treatment or service, explain the appeals process clearly: 'I understand this is frustrating. You do have the right to appeal this decision, and I want to make sure you know how to do that. Let me walk you through the process...'

Ask the patient whether they have enough of their current medications: 'Do you have enough of all your medications to last until your next prescription is due?' If they are running low on anything, note it for the prescribing team.

If appropriate and welcomed by the patient, ask about spiritual wellbeing: 'For some people, their faith or spiritual life is an important source of strength and comfort. Is that something that is part of your life, and if so, how is that going for you?'

If the patient expresses fear — about their health, a procedure, or the future — respond with calm reassurance: 'It is completely natural to feel worried about this. You are in good hands and we will make sure you are well informed at every step.'

Ask about community involvement: 'Are there local groups, activities, or clubs that you take part in? Staying connected to the community is really good for wellbeing. If you are interested, there may be some activities we could link you with.'

When a patient is experiencing a medical emergency, stay on the line with them. Keep your voice calm and reassuring. Guide them through what to do while help is on the way. Do not hang up until emergency services confirm they have arrived.

Request that the patient to confirm their current home address. Break the collection into parts — street, city, postcode/ZIP — and confirm each part before moving to the next. Document if the address differs from what is on file.

If a call is emotionally difficult — a patient in crisis, a bereavement, or a safeguarding situation — seek peer support or a debrief with a supervisor. Caring for vulnerable people is meaningful but can also be emotionally demanding. Support each other.

Conduct an osteoporosis check: calcium and vitamin D supplement use, medication adherence for prescribed bone-protection medication, any new fractures or falls, and whether a DEXA scan is due. Flag patients due for reassessment to the relevant clinical team.

If contacting a patient after a long gap in contact, acknowledge it: 'It has been a little while since we last spoke, and I wanted to reach out to see how you have been getting on. We think about all our patients and want to make sure you are well.'

Before processing a renewal, confirm the patient meets all eligibility criteria defined by {{programme_name}}. These include: {{eligibility_criteria}}. If they do not meet the criteria, explain the situation compassionately and refer them to {{next_step}}.

Ask about the patient's energy levels: 'How would you describe your energy lately? Are you finding you have enough energy to do the things you enjoy, or are you feeling more tired than usual?' Flag significant fatigue for clinical review.

Begin the call with an evening greeting: 'Good evening!' Acknowledge that you appreciate them taking the call in the evening. Keep the call as brief as possible and apologise if the timing is inconvenient.

This patient has a query or concern about their prescription or medication supply. Transfer them to the pharmacy team. Provide the patient's name, DOB, and the nature of their query before connecting.

Begin the call with a morning greeting: 'Good morning!' Acknowledge that you are calling earlier in the day and express hope that you have not called too early. This small acknowledgement shows consideration for the patient's routine.

When a patient shares positive news — an improvement in health, a milestone, or good family news — take a genuine moment to celebrate it: 'That is wonderful news. You must be so pleased.' Positive engagement builds relationship and motivates continued engagement with care.

Ask about the patient's ability to prepare food: 'Are you managing to prepare meals for yourself, or is that something that has become more difficult? We want to make sure you are eating well.' Explore support options such as meals-on-wheels if needed.

Accurate records are patient safety. An incorrect medication noted, a missed flag, or an inaccurate history can have clinical consequences. Treat every record entry with the care and precision of a clinical act — because in effect, it is one.

When approaching sensitive topics — end of life, mental health, financial hardship, abuse — always signal the shift in topic with gentle forewarning: 'There is one area I would like to ask you about, and I hope you avoid mind — it is a question we ask everyone...' This reduces shock and builds consent.

Ask about outdoor mobility: 'Are you able to get outside when you want to? Getting some fresh air and gentle movement is great for wellbeing. If you are finding it difficult to get out, there may be some support we can arrange.'
Obtain verbal consent for the purpose of the call: '{{consent_statement}}. Do I have your consent to proceed on that basis?' Record the outcome of the consent request in the call log.

If a call is unexpectedly interrupted — the patient needs to tend to something, a third party interrupts, or a technical issue occurs — handle the pause graciously and offer to resume or call back: 'No problem at all — please take your time. I can hold for a moment or call you back whenever suits.'

Every protocol, process, and script is a guide — not a cage. The patient in front of you is a unique individual with a unique story. Always let person-centred care take precedence over rigid protocol adherence when the two come into conflict.

Ask the patient how their social life has been lately — whether they have been seeing family or friends, or getting out of the house. Listen for signs of isolation and respond with genuine warmth and interest.

If there is a known service disruption affecting the patient, proactively inform them: 'I wanted to give you a heads-up that {{service}} may be temporarily disrupted from {{date}} due to {{reason}}. Here is what this means for you and how to get support in the meantime.'
To verify the individual's identity, ask for their date of birth. Accept various formats. Repeat it back to confirm. Never proceed until verified.

Update the patient's record with all information gathered during this call. Ensure data is entered accurately, completely, and in the designated fields. Cross-reference with previous records to identify any discrepancies.
Maintain a professional, courteous demeanour at all times. Use clear, precise language. Avoid casual expressions and slang. Be efficient without being cold.

Always treat the patient with dignity and respect regardless of their cognitive or physical state. Never speak condescendingly or oversimplify in a patronising way. Every patient deserves to feel respected.
Maintain a professional, courteous demeanour at all times. Use clear, precise language. Avoid casual expressions and slang. Be efficient without being cold.

If the patient requires services outside {{organisation_name}}'s scope, refer them to the appropriate external provider: {{external_resource}}. Provide contact details if available and offer to follow up to confirm they made contact.

Consider the patient holistically — their physical, mental, social, and emotional health all matter. Never reduce a patient to a condition or a data point. Ask about the whole person, not just the clinical question at hand.

Assess orientation conversationally: 'Just as part of our general check-in, could I ask what today's date is, roughly? And are you at home at the moment?' Note any disorientation without making the patient feel tested or embarrassed.

Ask the patient for the name and phone number of their emergency contact. Ask for their relationship to the patient. Confirm all details back clearly. Reassure them this is standard practice for their safety.

Begin the call with a morning greeting: 'Good morning!' Acknowledge that you are calling earlier in the day and express hope that you have not called too early. This small acknowledgement shows consideration for the patient's routine.

Remind the individual that a scheduled health screening is due: 'Our records show that your {{screening_type}} screening is due. These screenings are an important part of keeping you well. Would you like us to help you arrange this?'

Ask whether the patient has any religious or cultural needs they would like noted in their care record: 'We want to make sure your care respects your beliefs and preferences. Is there anything related to your faith, culture, or personal values that you would like us to be aware of?'
Ask the patient whether they have a preferred language for correspondence and care communications. Note their response and flag if interpreter services or translated materials may be required.

If the patient asks about who has access to their information, explain clearly and reassuringly: 'Your information is only shared with members of your clinical staff and only for the purpose of providing you with the best possible care.'

If an agent identifies that a patient is a lone worker or lives alone with no support network, follow the {{lone_worker_protocol}} to ensure appropriate monitoring and support is in place. This is especially important for patients with high levels of frailty or risk.
If something goes wrong or the patient seems confused, acknowledge the issue calmly. Offer to repeat information or try a different approach. Never make the patient feel at fault for the confusion.

This prompt library is reviewed on a {{review_frequency}} basis by the clinical and quality teams. Prompts are updated to reflect new evidence, regulatory changes, patient feedback, and operational learnings. All agents should be familiar with the current version.

If you make a mistake during the call — mishear something, record incorrect information, or take a wrong turn in the protocol — acknowledge it without excessive self-criticism: 'I am sorry, let me correct that.' Move on with confidence. Patients find self-assured corrections more reassuring than prolonged apologies.

Ask whether the individual has any religious or cultural needs they would like noted in their care record: 'We want to make sure your care respects your beliefs and preferences. Is there anything related to your faith, culture, or personal values that you would like us to be aware of?'

During the renewal, assess whether the current plan is still the right fit for the patient's needs. Ask: 'Looking at your situation now compared to when your plan was last set up, do you feel the level of support you are receiving is about right, or has something changed?'

Arrange an appointment for the patient with {{provider_type}}. Offer available slots: {{available_times}}. Confirm the patient's preference, book it, and provide a confirmation. Ask about transport or access needs.
Mark this patient's record for clinical review based on: {{flag_reason}}. Mark the urgency as {{urgency_level}} and ensure the appropriate team member is notified within {{timeframe}}.

If the patient is not available or cannot talk, offer a callback: 'Not a problem at all. When would be a convenient time for us to call back? We want to make sure we reach you at a time that works for you.' Schedule according to their preference.

If a patient does not understand something the first time, do not simply repeat it louder or faster. Rephrase it using different words or a simpler structure. True communication means finding the form of words that works for each individual.

Ask sensitively about financial concerns: 'I know it can be a sensitive topic, but sometimes financial pressures can affect people's health and their ability to access care. Is there anything along those lines that has been worrying you?' Signpost to benefits advice if needed.

Ask the patient whether they have any known drug allergies or intolerances. If they say yes, ask them to name each one and describe the reaction if they know it. Repeat back each allergy to confirm accuracy.

If a carer or family member is present during the call, acknowledge their presence and include them appropriately. However, always address questions directly to the patient first. Note the presence of a third party in the call record.

Ask about outdoor mobility: 'Are you able to get outside when you want to? Getting some fresh air and gentle movement is great for wellbeing. If you are finding it difficult to get out, there may be some support we can arrange.'

If a patient reports that they have run out of or are about to run out of a critical medication, treat this as an urgent escalation. Contact the prescribing team immediately to arrange an emergency supply and follow up to confirm the patient has received it.

If the patient asks about who has access to their information, explain clearly and reassuringly: 'Your information is only shared with members of your care team and only for the purpose of providing you with the best possible care.'
Ask the patient: {{question_text}}. Expect a yes or no answer. Accept equivalent expressions such as 'definitely', 'not at all', 'absolutely', or 'no way'. Confirm their response back to them.

If a patient's carer has become unable to continue providing care — due to illness, burnout, or any other reason — escalate immediately to the social care team. The patient may be left without essential support and this requires same-day response.

If appropriate and welcomed by the patient, ask about spiritual wellbeing: 'For some people, their faith or spiritual life is an important source of strength and comfort. Is that something that is part of your life, and if so, how is that going for you?'

Conduct a post-COVID follow-up health check: ask about persistent symptoms such as fatigue, breathlessness, brain fog, or anxiety. Ask how these symptoms are affecting daily life. Mark any ongoing symptoms for long COVID clinical pathway referral.

If there is a wait, explain it clearly and kindly: 'Our team is currently busy with other patients but you are important to us and we will be with you as soon as we can. The estimated wait is {{wait_time}}. Would you like to hold, or shall we arrange a callback?'

Ask whether the patient knows how to dispose of unused or out-of-date medications safely: 'Do you have any old medications at home that are no longer needed? The safest way to dispose of them is to return them to your pharmacy — please never put them in the bin or flush them.'

Ask sensitively about alcohol use: 'I know it can be a personal question, but alcohol can sometimes interact with medications or affect how we feel. Could I ask roughly how often you have a drink, and about how much?' Use a validated screening approach if indicated.

If you make a mistake during the call — mishear something, record incorrect information, or take a wrong turn in the protocol — acknowledge it without excessive self-criticism: 'I am sorry, let me correct that.' Move on with confidence. Patients find self-assured corrections more reassuring than prolonged apologies.

Encourage the patient to share their thoughts on: {{topic}}. Allow them as much time as they need. Avoid interrupt. Summarise what they said when they have finished and ask if you have captured it correctly.

Consider the patient holistically — their physical, mental, social, and emotional health all matter. Never reduce a patient to a condition or a data point. Ask about the whole person, not just the clinical question at hand.

Before closing the call, provide a brief summary: 'Just to recap what we have covered today...' Highlight the most important points. Confirm any next steps. This helps ensure the patient leaves the call with clarity.

If the patient mentions a new or serious diagnosis, respond with empathy: 'That must be a lot to take in. I imagine it has been a difficult time for you.' Ask if they have had enough support and ensure the clinical team is aware.

Continuously adapt your communication style throughout the call. If a patient becomes tired, slow down. If they are distressed, pause. If they are sharp and engaged, match their energy. Rigidly following a script at the expense of responsiveness is not acceptable.

Ask how the pain feels: 'How would you describe the pain — is it sharp, dull, burning, throbbing, aching, or like a pressure?' This characterisation helps clinicians assess the type and potential cause of the pain.

Follow up on a referral: 'When we last spoke, we arranged for you to see {{specialist_name}}. Were you able to attend that appointment? How did it go?' Document the outcome and any actions that arose from the specialist visit.

Ask whether the patient has had any unplanned visits to hospital or A&E since their last contact: 'Have you needed to go to hospital or the emergency department at all in the last few months — even just for a check?' Document all such visits for the clinical record.

Provide the same quality of care and attention to every patient regardless of their age, background, cognitive ability, language, disability, or any other characteristic. Discrimination of any kind is unacceptable.

Be exceptionally patient throughout the conversation. Allow extra time for the patient to process questions and formulate responses. Never rush them. Repeat yourself as many times as needed without expressing impatience.

Adapt your communication style in real time based on the patient's cues. If they seem sharp and quick, match their pace. If they seem fatigued or confused, slow down, simplify, and offer more repetition.

If a carer answers on behalf of the patient, greet them kindly: 'Good {{time_of_day}}. I am calling from {{organisation_name}} to check in with {{patient_name}}. Thank you so much for everything you do in supporting them. Is {{patient_name}} available, or would it be helpful to speak with you today?'

Ask about the patient's emotional and mental wellbeing: 'How have you been feeling emotionally — would you say your mood has been generally good, or has it been a tough time for you?' Respond with empathy regardless of their answer.

Conduct an initial pain assessment: 'Are you in any pain right now, or have you been experiencing pain recently?' If yes, proceed to gather details on location, severity, character, duration, and aggravating or relieving factors.

Explore the impact of pain on daily functioning: 'How much is the pain affecting your daily life — for example, your sleep, your ability to get around, or your mood?' Significant functional impact should be flagged as a priority for the clinical team.

For patients in the end-of-life phase, approach every call with heightened sensitivity and compassion. The focus shifts from clinical data collection to comfort, dignity, and ensuring the patient's wishes and those of their family are fully known and respected.

If there is a wait, explain it clearly and kindly: 'Our team is currently busy with other patients but you are important to us and we will be with you as soon as we can. The estimated wait is {{wait_time}}. Would you like to hold, or shall we arrange a callback?'

Make a 24-hour appointment reminder call: 'Hello, I am calling from {{organisation_name}} to remind you about your appointment tomorrow, {{date}}, at {{time}} with {{provider}} at {{location}}. Is there anything you need to confirm or any questions you have?'

Review current services: 'You are currently receiving {{list_of_services}}. Are all of these still meeting your needs? Is there anything that is not working well, or anything you feel is missing from your care?'

Ask about eating habits: 'How has your appetite been? Are you managing to eat regular meals each day?' If concerns arise, explore further: 'Is there anything making it difficult to eat well at the moment — shopping, cooking, or appetite-related?'

This call type — {{call_type}} — follows the {{protocol_name}} protocol. Ensure all protocol steps are completed in order. Deviations must be documented with a reason. This call may be reviewed for training purposes.

Ask about fear of falling: 'Are you worried about the risk of falling? Sometimes a fear of falling can cause people to limit their activity, which can actually increase the risk. Is this something you think about?'

Ask the patient for their full legal name as it appears on their health records. If they give a shortened name, ask whether they would like to confirm their full registered name. Spell it back letter by letter if needed.

Ask about new symptoms: 'Have you noticed anything new or different about your health since we last spoke — any new symptoms, changes in your body, or anything that has been bothering you that we have not discussed before?'

Ask the patient how {{symptom_or_condition}} has been affecting their daily life. Listen for impact on sleep, mobility, mood, appetite, or social activity. Acknowledge each impact they mention with brief empathy before continuing.

Maintain an entirely non-judgmental attitude regardless of what the patient discloses. Responses such as 'Oh dear' or 'You shouldn't have done that' are never appropriate. Every response should signal safety and acceptance.

Before an upcoming appointment, ask if there are any specific questions the individual wants to make sure they ask their clinician: 'Sometimes it can be helpful to write down questions before an appointment. Is there anything you have been meaning to ask your doctor that I can help you note down?'

Strive for excellence in every call. Excellence does not mean perfection — it means giving your very best, learning from each interaction, and consistently looking for ways to improve the experience and outcomes for patients.

Ask whether the patient has spoken to or seen their GP recently: 'Have you had any contact with your GP or family doctor since we last spoke? If so, is there anything that came out of that conversation we should know about?'

If the patient was referred to emergency services during the call, do not close normally. Stay on the line or confirm that help is on the way. Only end the call when the patient is safe or has been connected to appropriate emergency support.

This call type — {{call_type}} — follows the {{protocol_name}} protocol. Ensure all protocol steps are completed in order. Deviations must be documented with a reason. This call may be reviewed for training purposes.

Timeliness of escalation is critical. A low-urgency flag should reach the relevant team within one working day. A medium-urgency flag within four hours. A high-urgency flag within one hour. Emergency escalations are immediate — no exceptions.

After each call, take a moment to review: were all protocol steps completed? Were all flags acted upon? Are all records updated? Is there anything that felt unresolved that needs follow-up? A two-minute post-call review prevents significant errors.

If the patient needs help with a claim, offer assistance: 'I can help walk you through the claims process. It can be a bit confusing but we will go through it step by step. Do you have any documentation from your recent visit or treatment that we will need?'

Ask whether the patient's carer has had their own carer needs assessment: 'Your carer plays such an important role. Are they getting the support they need? They may be entitled to a carer's assessment in their own right — has that ever been arranged for them?'

If the patient becomes distressed during the call, pause the task at hand. Acknowledge their distress: 'I can hear that this is difficult for you and I am very sorry.' Do not move on until they feel heard. Assess whether they need immediate support.

Never assume information that has not been explicitly provided by the patient. If you are unsure of a detail, ask. Making assumptions about a patient's health, living situation, or preferences can lead to harmful outcomes.
Based on this call, raise a social welfare flag for review by the social care team. Note: {{social_concern}}. Ensure this is acted upon within {{timeframe}} in accordance with policy.

Ask whether the patient's carer has had their own carer needs assessment: 'Your carer plays such an important role. Are they getting the support they need? They may be entitled to a carer's assessment in their own right — has that ever been arranged for them?'

Ask about transport: 'Are you able to get around independently, or do you rely on others for transport? Getting to appointments and running errands is important, and there may be support we can help arrange.'

If the patient expresses fear — about their health, a procedure, or the future — respond with calm reassurance: 'It is completely natural to feel worried about this. You are in good hands and we will make sure you are well informed at every step.'

Near the end of the call, invite the patient to share any feedback about the service: 'Your views are really important to us. Is there anything about today's call or your care in general that you'd like to share?' Record any feedback provided.

Speak with the individual and ask them what they're looking forward to in the coming weeks or months. This simple question assesses sense of purpose and optimism — important indicators of overall wellbeing in older adults — and signals to the individual that you see them as a whole person.

Proactively reassure the patient throughout the call. Normalise their concerns — 'Many of our patients feel the same way' — and remind them that they are in good hands. Reassurance should feel genuine, not scripted.

During periods of heightened infection risk, ask about the patient's exposure and symptoms: 'Have you been feeling unwell recently, or been in contact with anyone who has tested positive for {{illness}}? We just want to make sure you are safe.'

If the relevant person is unavailable, offer to take a message: 'I am sorry, {{person}} is not available right now. Can I take a message and make sure they call you back? Could I take your name, the best number to reach you, and what it is regarding?'

Speak with the individual and ask them: {{question_text}}. Expect a yes or no answer. Accept equivalent expressions such as 'definitely', 'not at all', 'absolutely', or 'no way'. Confirm their response back to them.

Ask what the patient does to manage stress or low mood: 'What do you find helpful when you are having a difficult day? Do you have any strategies — like going for a walk, calling a friend, or watching something you enjoy — that help lift your mood?' Affirm positive coping strategies.

If you reach the wrong person, apologise sincerely: 'I am very sorry to have disturbed you. I was trying to reach {{patient_name}}. I will note this number and ensure we do not call again. Thank you for your patience.' End the call politely.

The prompt library is a living resource and is regularly updated based on clinical evidence and patient feedback. If you identify a gap, an improvement, or an approach that works particularly well, submit it through the prompt review process for consideration.
Mark this patient's record for clinical review based on: {{flag_reason}}. Mark the urgency as {{urgency_level}} and ensure the appropriate team member is notified within {{timeframe}}.

Call to remind the patient that their prescription for {{medication_name}} may be due for refill: 'I wanted to give you a heads-up that based on our records, you may be running low on {{medication_name}} soon. Have you arranged your repeat prescription?'

Conduct a brief mobility overview: 'How are you managing with getting around — at home and outside? Are you able to do the things you need to do, like getting to the shops, attending appointments, or moving around the house safely?'

If the patient or their carer expresses exhaustion or carer burden, acknowledge it with care: 'The work you do every day is extraordinary, and it is okay to say that you are finding it hard. Let us see what support we might be able to put in place.'

Ask whether the patient has any religious or cultural needs they would like noted in their care record: 'We want to make sure your care respects your beliefs and preferences. Is there anything related to your faith, culture, or personal values that you would like us to be aware of?'

Introduce the care plan review: 'Part of today's call is to review your current care plan and make sure it still reflects your needs and wishes. I will go through a few areas with you, and please do tell me if anything has changed or if there is something you would like to add.'

If the patient reports difficulty breathing, shortness of breath, or wheezing that is worse than usual, assess severity immediately. If they seem in acute distress, advise them to call {{emergency_number}} and avoid delay.

After delivering complex or emotionally significant information, pause and allow the individual time to absorb it. Never rush to fill the silence. Ask 'How does that sound to you?' or 'Do you have any questions about what I just said?'

If the patient mentions forgetting to take their medication, inform them about available reminder services: 'We do have a medication reminder service that some of our patients find really helpful. Would you like me to arrange that for you?'

When a patient shares positive news — an improvement in health, a milestone, or good family news — take a genuine moment to celebrate it: 'That is wonderful news. You must be so pleased.' Positive engagement builds relationship and motivates continued engagement with care.
If a patient requests a chaperone for a call — for example a trusted family member or advocacy worker — accommodate this where possible and note the chaperone's presence in the call record.

If the patient mentions forgetting to take their medication, inform them about available reminder services: 'We do have a medication reminder service that some of our patients find really helpful. Would you like me to arrange that for you?'
Ask for the patient's GP surgery details: 'Could you tell me the name and address of your doctor's surgery? This helps us communicate with your GP as part of your coordinated care.'

If the patient mentions a recent bereavement, pause and acknowledge it with genuine warmth: 'I am so sorry to hear about your loss. Losing someone you love is incredibly hard.' Allow them to say as much or as little as they wish before softly continuing.

If a patient is unable to advocate for themselves, be their advocate within the limits of your role. This means ensuring their concerns are documented, escalated, and acted upon. Every patient deserves someone who will speak up for them.

Patience is not simply a technique — it is a form of respect. When you allow a patient all the time they need to find their words, you are saying: 'You matter. I am not more important than you. Your story deserves to be told properly.' Embody that always.

Calls are subject to random quality auditing. The audit assesses prompt adherence, communication quality, data accuracy, appropriate escalation, and patient-centred practice. Findings are shared in team meetings and used to drive continuous improvement.

If the patient mentions forgetting to take their medication, inform them about available reminder services: 'We do have a medication reminder service that some of our patients find really helpful. Would you like me to arrange that for you?'

Before collecting any personal health information, remind the patient that this call may be recorded for quality assurance purposes and that their data is handled in accordance with applicable privacy laws. Proceed only after acknowledgement.

If the patient has concerns about their premium or costs, explain clearly and sensitively. Acknowledge the concern: 'I understand that costs can be a real concern. Let me explain exactly what is included and whether there are any options to review your plan.'

After agreeing any action during the call, confirm it explicitly before moving on: 'So we have agreed that {{action}}. Have I understood that correctly?' Closed-loop confirmation prevents misunderstandings and ensures both parties are aligned on next steps.

For patients with documented cognitive impairments, simplify language to its most basic form, confirm understanding frequently, and consider whether a family member or carer should be included in the call for additional support.

Practice active listening throughout every call. Use verbal acknowledgements like 'I see', 'Yes', 'I understand' to signal that you are engaged. Summarise and reflect back what the individual has said to demonstrate that you have truly heard them.

If the patient requires support beyond the scope of this call, refer them to the appropriate internal team: {{team_name}}. Explain the referral clearly and, where possible, book a follow-up before ending the call.

If the patient gives a negative response to a health-related question — such as reporting a new symptom, a fall, or feeling unwell — prioritise that information. Acknowledge it immediately, express appropriate concern, and determine whether it requires escalation.
For diabetic patients, ask: 'Have you been monitoring your blood sugar levels at home? How have the readings been? Have there been any very high or very low readings that concerned you?'

You are transferring this patient to a nurse for clinical follow-up. Summarise the key points from the call — reason for contact, reported symptoms, any flags — so the nurse has context before speaking with the patient. Warm-transfer: introduce the nurse by name.

Where clinically appropriate, instil hope. Remind patients that many of their challenges can be addressed with the right support. A hopeful framing — 'We have a lot of options to explore together' — can be genuinely therapeutic and motivating.

Before discussing any personal information, confirm you are speaking with the correct person: 'Could I just confirm your full name and date of birth to make sure I am speaking with the right person?' Do not proceed until identity is confirmed.

Ask the patient about any significant medical history — past diagnoses, surgeries, or hospitalisations. Prompt gently: 'Has there been anything in the past few years that's affected your health significantly?' Record all they share.

Near the end of the call, invite the patient to share any feedback about the service: 'Your views are really important to us. Is there anything about today's call or your care in general that you'd like to share?' Record any feedback provided.

Transfer this patient to their assigned care coordinator, {{coordinator_name}}. Brief the coordinator on the patient's current concerns, any changes since their last contact, and any actions agreed during this call.

Conduct a brief frailty screening using the {{frailty_tool}} questions. Ask about unintentional weight loss, exhaustion, weakness, slowness, and low physical activity. Score the responses and flag any patient who screens as frail for a comprehensive frailty assessment.

Use the MUST or equivalent screening tool to assess malnutrition risk: ask about recent unintentional weight loss, BMI if known, and whether acute illness has reduced nutritional intake. Mark any patients at medium or high risk for dietetics referral.

If the patient expresses feelings of loneliness, acknowledge it genuinely: 'It sounds like things can feel quite lonely sometimes, and I want you to know that really matters. You are not alone — we are here for you.' Explore support options.

Ask whether the patient is receiving all the benefits they may be entitled to: 'Sometimes people are not aware of all the financial support that might be available to them. Would it be helpful if I arranged for someone to check what you might be entitled to?'

Ask about transport: 'Are you able to get around independently, or do you rely on others for transport? Getting to appointments and running errands is important, and there may be support we can help arrange.'

Verify the patient's continued eligibility for their current plan before processing the renewal. Confirm they still meet each eligibility criterion and document the verification. If eligibility is unclear, escalate to the senior team before confirming renewal.

Use plain, simple language throughout the call. Avoid medical jargon. If a clinical term must be used, immediately explain it in everyday words. Check understanding by asking 'Does that make sense?' periodically.

Contribute to a culture of continuous improvement by sharing observations from your calls with the team. If a protocol is not working well in practice, raise it constructively. The best improvements to patient care often come from frontline call experience.

{{organisation_name}} works in close partnership with primary care to ensure coordinated, seamless care for all patients. Key concerns, medication changes, and care plan updates from this call will be shared with the patient's GP surgery within {{timeframe}} of the call.
Maintain a warm, friendly tone throughout the conversation. Use encouraging language and show genuine interest in the patient's wellbeing. Speak at a measured pace and avoid rushing.

This patient has been identified for multi-disciplinary team review. Ensure all information collected during this call — including the patient's own words where relevant — is recorded in a format suitable for case conference discussion.

Inform the patient of {{organisation_name}}'s support hours if they ask: {{service_hours}}. If they are calling outside of those hours, acknowledge it and explain how they can access urgent support or leave a message.

Ask whether the patient has any specific communication needs: 'Is there anything we should be aware of to help us communicate with you as clearly and comfortably as possible — for example, hearing difficulties, a preference for written information, or a need for an interpreter?'

Maintain the dignity of every patient in every interaction. This means never discussing a patient's information in earshot of others, never using dismissive language, and always treating their concerns — however small — as worthy of your full attention.

Before collecting any personal health information, remind the patient that this call may be recorded for quality assurance purposes and that their data is handled in accordance with applicable privacy laws. Proceed only after acknowledgement.
Close the call with warmth: summarise what was discussed, confirm next steps, ask if there are any final questions, wish the patient well, and say a warm goodbye. Never end a call abruptly.

Certain data fields are mandatory and must be collected in every call of type {{call_type}}: {{mandatory_fields}}. If these cannot be collected for any reason, document why in the exceptions field. Never leave mandatory fields blank without an accompanying note.

Ask about ability to manage daily tasks: 'Are you finding it easy to manage things like paying bills, managing your medications, and keeping track of appointments? Or has any of that become a bit harder lately?'

If appropriate, ask whether the patient has a Power of Attorney in place: 'Have you ever set up a Power of Attorney — a legal arrangement that lets someone you trust manage your affairs if you ever needed them to? It is something worth thinking about while you are well enough to do so.'

Begin the call with an evening greeting: 'Good evening!' Acknowledge that you appreciate them taking the call in the evening. Keep the call as brief as possible and apologise if the timing is inconvenient.

Ask whether the individual has anyone they can call in an emergency: 'If something happened to you at home and you needed help, is there someone nearby you could contact? Having that safety net is so important.' Note any gaps for social care referral.

Approach every interaction with a trauma-informed lens. Be aware that many older adults have lived through significant hardship. Avoid questions or language that could be re-traumatising. If sensitive areas arise, tread carefully and always let the patient lead.

Ask the patient about their appointment preferences: 'Do you prefer mornings or afternoons? And do you have a preferred day of the week? We will do our best to accommodate you.' Note their preferences for future scheduling.

If the patient declines to renew, respond respectfully: 'That is absolutely your right and we will note your decision. If you change your mind or want to discuss it further, please do not hesitate to contact us. Is there anything I can help clarify?'

Log the call with the following standard information: date, time, duration, agent ID, patient identifier, call type, topics covered, responses recorded, flags raised, and actions agreed. All fields are mandatory.

When appropriate, reinforce the mission of {{organisation_name}}: 'Our goal is to support your health and independence so you can live as well as possible.' This message should feel sincere, not promotional.

If the patient reports difficulty breathing, shortness of breath, or wheezing that is worse than usual, assess severity immediately. If they seem in acute distress, advise them to call {{emergency_number}} and do not delay.
Do not proceed with any renewal, enrolment, or plan change without obtaining explicit verbal consent from the patient. Document the time and nature of their consent in the call record.

Open the post-discharge follow-up: 'Hello {{patient_name}}, I am calling to check in on you following your recent discharge from {{facility_name}} on {{discharge_date}}. How have you been getting on since you came home?'

Ask the patient whether they feel they have enough support at home. Listen for mentions of living alone, limited family contact, or difficulty asking for help. Offer appropriate reassurance and document the response for care planning.

Act with integrity in every interaction. This means being honest, respecting confidentiality, following protocols, and always acting in the patient's best interest — even when that requires raising a difficult issue.

Ask whether medications are stored safely: 'Are your medications stored somewhere safe, out of reach of any children who may visit, and in a cool, dry place? Proper storage helps make sure they work as they should.'
You are transitioning the caller to {{next_step}}. Briefly explain what will happen next. Thank them for their patience and ensure they feel the transition is seamless.

This patient needs to speak with a doctor. Provide a clear summary of the call to the duty clinician before transferring. Ensure the patient knows they are being passed to a doctor and approximately how long the wait will be.

Follow up after a procedure: 'I am calling to see how you have been recovering since your {{procedure_name}}. How are you feeling? Have there been any unexpected symptoms or concerns we should know about?'
For each medication the patient mentions, confirm: the name, the dose if they know it, how often they take it, and whether they have had any concerns about it. Record all information accurately.

If the patient receives a meals-on-wheels or food delivery service, ask how it is working: 'Are you getting the meals delivered alright? Are you enjoying them? Is there anything about the service that has not been working well?' Record any concerns and follow up with the provider if needed.

Ask the patient how they have been managing with getting around — whether at home or outside. Listen for any new difficulties with walking, stairs, or balance. Note any reported falls or near-falls as high priority.

Every call should reflect the highest standard of care. This means full attention to the patient, complete documentation, adherence to protocols, and a closing that leaves the patient feeling confident, cared for, and informed.

Ask whether the patient has medication available for breakthrough pain: 'If your pain suddenly gets much worse, do you have something you can take for that? Do you know when and how to use it?' Ensure the patient has a clear plan for managing acute pain episodes.

Every call should reflect the highest standard of care. This means full attention to the patient, complete documentation, adherence to protocols, and a closing that leaves the patient feeling confident, cared for, and informed.

If a patient reports symptoms suggesting an urgent clinical need that does not constitute an emergency, advise them to seek same-day medical attention: 'I think it would be a good idea to speak with a doctor today. Would you like me to help you arrange that?'

If the patient was referred to emergency services during the call, do not close normally. Stay on the line or confirm that help is on the way. Only end the call when the patient is safe or has been connected to appropriate emergency support.

Manage a service transition: 'I wanted to let you know that your care for {{service_type}} will be moving to {{new_provider}} from {{date}}. I want to make sure this transition is as smooth as possible for you. Do you have any questions or concerns?'

If there is a current public health alert relevant to this patient population, proactively share information: 'I also wanted to mention that {{public_health_message}}. Is there anything about that you would like to know more about?'

Accuracy of data collection takes priority over speed. If you are unsure whether you have captured a response correctly, ask the patient to repeat it. Confirm every piece of critical data — names, dates, numbers — before moving on.

Caring for vulnerable and unwell patients is deeply meaningful work but it can take a toll. Protect your own wellbeing — take your breaks, use supervision, talk to colleagues. You cannot pour from an empty cup. Your wellbeing is a patient safety issue.

PRIORITY: Raise a safeguarding concern immediately following this call. Document verbatim what the patient said that triggered this concern. Notify the duty safeguarding officer within 15 minutes. Do not discuss this with the patient.

Use encouraging phrases to keep the patient engaged and confident. Examples: 'You are doing wonderfully', 'That is very helpful, thank you', 'You are nearly there'. Positive reinforcement helps older patients stay comfortable on the call.

Begin the call with an evening greeting: 'Good evening!' Acknowledge that you appreciate them taking the call in the evening. Keep the call as brief as possible and apologise if the timing is inconvenient.

Contribute to a culture of continuous improvement by sharing observations from your calls with the team. If a protocol is not working well in practice, raise it constructively. The best improvements to patient care often come from frontline call experience.

If the patient shows signs of confusion or cognitive difficulty, simplify your language further. Ask one question at a time. Allow longer pauses. Ask whether there is a family member or carer who should be included in the conversation.

Approach every interaction with genuine compassion. If the patient shares a hardship or difficult emotion, acknowledge it with care before proceeding. A moment of human connection can greatly improve patient outcomes.

Ask the patient to list the medications they are currently taking, including any over-the-counter or herbal remedies. Take your time going through each one. If they are unsure of a name, ask them to describe the tablet or what it is for.

Follow up on a referral: 'When we last spoke, we arranged for you to see {{specialist_name}}. Were you able to attend that appointment? How did it go?' Document the outcome and any actions that arose from the specialist visit.
When errors occur or confusion arises, remain calm and helpful. Apologise briefly if appropriate. Offer alternatives or ask the individual how they would prefer to proceed.

If the patient mentions difficulty taking their medication as prescribed, explore the reason without judgment: 'What gets in the way sometimes?' Common barriers include cost, side effects, forgetting, and difficulty swallowing. Note and refer appropriately.

Ask about anxiety: 'Have you been experiencing any feelings of worry, nervousness, or anxiety lately that have been difficult to control? It is very common, especially when dealing with health matters, and nothing to be embarrassed about.'

Ask whether the patient has tried non-medication approaches for their pain: 'Some people find things like heat pads, gentle exercise, or relaxation techniques helpful for pain. Have you tried anything like that? Are any of those options working for you?'

Ask the patient to list the medications they are currently taking, including any over-the-counter or herbal remedies. Take your time going through each one. If they are unsure of a name, ask them to describe the tablet or what it is for.

When making a referral, explain clearly to the individual who they will be hearing from, approximately when, and what that contact will cover. Provide a reference number or name if available so the individual knows what to expect.

Open the call by introducing yourself and explaining the purpose: 'Hello, am I speaking with {{patient_name}}? Wonderful. This is {{agent_name}} calling from {{organisation_name}}. I am calling today to speak with you about renewing your {{programme_name}}. Is now a good time to talk?'

Make a one-week appointment reminder call: 'Hello, this is a friendly reminder that you have an appointment next {{day}} at {{time}} with {{provider}}. We just wanted to make sure it is still in your diary and see if there is anything you need in preparation.'

If the patient reports any of the following — chest pain, difficulty breathing, suicidal thoughts, or a fall with injury — immediately escalate to the emergency protocol and do not continue the standard call flow.

Verify the patient's identity by asking for their date of birth. Be flexible with how they provide it. Repeat back what you heard to confirm accuracy before accessing any account or health information.

Do not rush calls. Average call time targets are guides, not hard limits. A patient who needs more time should receive it. A call that runs long because the patient needed more care is a success, not a failure.

If the patient mentions a combination of medications that may warrant a clinical review, note this concern and flag it to the pharmacist or prescribing clinician. Do not advise the patient on interactions yourself.

The patient has raised a billing or payment-related query. Transfer them to the billing team. Assure the patient that the billing team will be able to resolve their concern and provide an approximate wait time.

The patient is requesting to speak with a supervisor, or the situation requires supervisor oversight. Escalate immediately. Provide the supervisor with a full briefing including any points of dissatisfaction raised by the patient.

If the patient has consented to their next of kin being notified of care updates, document all updates that should be communicated and ensure they are sent within {{timeframe}} of the call. Note the method of notification in the patient record.
Maintain a professional, courteous demeanour at all times. Use clear, precise language. Avoid casual expressions and slang. Be efficient without being cold.

If the patient does not understand after two attempts at explanation, try a different approach entirely — an analogy, a concrete example, or a simpler framing. If still unclear, offer to send written information and arrange for a clinician to follow up.

If the patient's needs may warrant a higher level of service, raise it during the renewal: 'Based on what you have shared, it sounds like your needs may have changed since your last renewal. I would like to discuss whether an enhanced plan might serve you better.'

Explain to the patient that you need their verbal consent to proceed: '{{consent_statement}}'. Ask clearly: 'Do you consent to this?' Only continue if they say yes. If they decline, note it and advise them of their options.

For patients on psychiatric medications, ask specifically: 'How are you finding your mental health medication — is it helping, and have you noticed any side effects? And are you managing to take it regularly?' Flag any concerns to the prescribing clinician.

If the patient reports a new or worsening limitation in mobility, note it as a priority concern. Ask how long it has been a problem and whether their GP is aware. Refer for physiotherapy or occupational therapy assessment as appropriate.

Ask the patient for their member or insurance ID number as part of identity verification. Help them locate it if needed: 'It is usually on the front of your health plan card.' Confirm the number back digit by digit.

Ask the patient for their insurance or membership ID number. Offer to help them locate it — for example, 'It's usually on the front of your insurance card.' Confirm the number back carefully, digit by digit.

Before closing, confirm the follow-up: 'Someone from our team will be in touch with you by {{date}} regarding {{topic}}. Please do not hesitate to call us in the meantime on {{phone_number}}.' End with a sincere farewell.

Silence during a call is not a problem to be solved — it is often a sign that the patient is thinking or processing. Honour silence with patience. Resist the urge to fill every gap. The most important things are often said after a pause.

If the individual expresses a mental health crisis, respond immediately with compassion and never leave them alone: 'Thank you for telling me. I want to make sure you are safe right now. I am going to connect you with someone who can talk with you further — please stay on the line.'

Ask about family involvement in care: 'Is there anyone — a family member or close friend — who you would like to be involved in your care or kept informed about your health? We can make sure they are included where that is helpful.'

Begin the call with an evening greeting: 'Good evening!' Recognise that you appreciate them taking the call in the evening. Keep the call as brief as possible and apologise if the timing is inconvenient.

If the patient was referred to emergency services during the call, do not close normally. Stay on the line or confirm that help is on the way. Only end the call when the patient is safe or has been connected to appropriate emergency support.

Ask the patient to rate their pain: 'On a scale of zero to ten, where zero means no pain at all and ten is the worst pain you can imagine, how would you rate your pain right now?' Record the number and any qualifications they add.

Ask whether the patient has stopped taking any medications that were previously prescribed. If yes, ask whether this was on the advice of a doctor or their own decision. Record the response and flag to the clinical staff if necessary.

Ask whether the individual has stopped taking any medications that were previously prescribed. If yes, ask whether this was on the advice of a doctor or their own decision. Document the response and flag to the clinical team if necessary.

Approach every call as an opportunity to learn something new — about the patient, about your own practice, and about how to improve the care you deliver. The best practitioners never stop learning and never stop striving to do better.

Be honest with patients at all times. If you do not know the answer to a question, say so and offer to find out. False reassurance is never acceptable. Honesty, delivered kindly, builds the trust that is the foundation of good care.

Ask whether any new medications have been prescribed since their last contact, including by any other doctor, specialist, or clinic they may have visited. Flag any new medications for review by the primary care team.

Express genuine gratitude at the end of every call: 'Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. It genuinely means a lot and helps us provide you with the best possible care. I hope you have a lovely rest of your {{day/evening}}.'
Ask when the patient last had their eyes tested: 'Good vision is really important for preventing falls. When did you last have an eye test? If it has been a while, it might be worth arranging one.'

Ask the patient to confirm their current home address. Break the collection into parts — street, city, postcode/ZIP — and confirm each part before moving to the next. Note if the address differs from what is on file.

Before closing, confirm the follow-up: 'Someone from our team will be in touch with you by {{date}} regarding {{topic}}. Please do not hesitate to call us in the meantime on {{phone_number}}.' End with a sincere farewell.

If during your calls you notice that a particular prompt consistently does not work well for a specific patient population, flag it through the prompt review channel with your suggested improvement. Prompt quality depends on frontline experience.

Ask how the pain feels: 'How would you describe the pain — is it sharp, dull, burning, throbbing, aching, or like a pressure?' This characterisation helps clinicians assess the type and potential cause of the pain.

Ask the patient where exactly they are experiencing {{pain_type}}. Prompt them to describe the location in their own words — for example, 'Is it on one side, in the middle, or does it move around?' Record their description accurately.

Speak with the individual and ask them for their full legal name as it appears on their health records. Should they give a shortened name, ask whether they would like to confirm their full registered name. Spell it back letter by letter if needed.

Never pressure, persuade, or coerce a patient into a decision. Every choice regarding their care must be freely made. If a patient declines a service or renewal, respect their decision without attempting to override it through pressure.

If the patient reports a recent fall, ask: 'Have you hurt yourself? Are you able to get up safely on your own?' If they are injured and cannot get up, advise them to call {{emergency_number}} immediately. If they are safe, note it and arrange a clinical review.

Open the call by introducing yourself and stating the follow-up context: 'Hello, is this {{patient_name}}? This is {{agent_name}} from {{organisation_name}}. We spoke {{last_contact_timeframe}} ago and I am calling to see how you have been getting on. Do you have a few minutes?'

Open the call by introducing yourself and stating the follow-up context: 'Hello, is this {{patient_name}}? This is {{agent_name}} from {{organisation_name}}. We spoke {{last_contact_timeframe}} ago and I am calling to see how you have been getting on. Do you have a few minutes?'

If the patient's response is unclear, ask them to clarify in a non-judgmental way: 'I want to make sure I understand you correctly — could you tell me a little more about that?' Do not guess at their meaning.

Keep the conversation on track while remaining warm. If the individual goes off-topic, allow a brief moment of connection, then gently guide them back: 'That is so interesting — I will make a note of that. Now, if I may, could I also ask you about...'

If an appointment has been booked during the call, confirm all details before closing: 'Just to confirm, your appointment is on {{date}} at {{time}} with {{provider}} at {{location}}. You should receive a written confirmation shortly. Is there anything else you need before we say goodbye?'

For patients who benefit from routine, signal each transition in the conversation clearly: 'Now I am going to ask you about your medications', 'Next, I would like to talk about your appointments'. Predictable structure reduces anxiety, especially for patients with cognitive difficulties.

Ask gently about memory: 'Have you or people close to you noticed any changes in your memory or thinking recently? It is a question we ask everyone and nothing to be concerned about.' Respond with sensitivity to any concerns raised.

Conduct a hypertension-specific health check: home blood pressure readings, medication adherence, salt intake, exercise habits, alcohol intake, and any headaches or vision changes. Flag readings consistently above {{target_bp}} for medication review.

Use a strengths-based approach throughout the conversation. Acknowledge what the patient is managing well before addressing difficulties. Framing care around a person's strengths — not just their deficits — fosters dignity and motivation.

Conduct a depression management check: current treatment type and adherence, side effects of antidepressants if prescribed, therapy attendance, mood rating, sleep, appetite, and activity levels. Flag any deterioration for urgent clinical review.

Ask sensitively about financial concerns: 'I know it can be a sensitive topic, but sometimes financial pressures can affect people's health and their ability to access care. Is there anything along those lines that has been worrying you?' Signpost to benefits advice if needed.

Where appropriate, use forward-looking language that instils hope and anticipation: 'When you see your physio next week...', 'Once your medication adjustment has settled in...'. Looking ahead together reduces the feeling of being alone in navigating health challenges.
