Introduce the renewal: 'The purpose of today's call is to go through your renewal for {{programme_name}}. This involves a few questions to make sure everything is still up to date, and then we will confirm whether you are eligible to continue. It usually takes about {{duration}} minutes.'

If the patient fails identity verification, do not share any personal information. Apologise calmly: 'I am sorry, I am not able to confirm the details you have provided. For your security, I am unable to continue. Could you call back with your ID card handy?' Never suggest what the correct answer is.

If an agent identifies that a patient is a lone worker or lives alone with no support network, follow the {{lone_worker_protocol}} to ensure appropriate monitoring and support is in place. This is especially important for patients with high levels of frailty or risk.

Conduct a post-COVID follow-up health check: ask about persistent symptoms such as fatigue, breathlessness, brain fog, or anxiety. Ask how these symptoms are affecting daily life. Flag any ongoing symptoms for long COVID clinical pathway referral.

If a patient reports that they have run out of or are about to run out of a critical medication, treat this as an urgent escalation. Contact the prescribing team immediately to arrange an emergency supply and follow up to confirm the patient has received it.

During the winter months, conduct a seasonal check: 'How are you managing with the cold weather? Are you keeping warm at home? Are you aware of any help available with heating costs if that is a concern?' Flag heating hardship to the social care team.

Follow up on pending lab results: 'I am calling to let you know that the results of your recent {{test_name}} are now available. Your doctor will be reviewing them and will be in touch if there is anything that needs to be discussed. Is there anything you would like to ask in the meantime?'

Practice active listening throughout every call. Use verbal acknowledgements like 'I see', 'Yes', 'I understand' to signal that you are engaged. Summarise and reflect back what the patient has said to demonstrate that you have truly heard them.

Use formal modes of address throughout the call. Refer to the individual as Mr., Mrs., Ms., or Dr. {{last_name}} unless they ask you to use their first name. Maintain respectful, dignified language at all times.

Always use plain language. Never assume a patient understands medical terminology. When in doubt, explain. The goal is for the patient to leave the call fully understanding what was discussed and what happens next.

Confirm the patient's identity by asking for their full name and date of birth. Both must match records before any personal health information can be shared. If there is a discrepancy, do not proceed and refer to the verification error protocol.

Ask whether the patient has anyone they can call in an emergency: 'If something happened to you at home and you needed help, is there someone nearby you could contact? Having that safety net is so important.' Document any gaps for social care referral.

Ask the patient whether they have experienced any changes in how they are feeling since their medications were last reviewed. Offer prompts if needed: 'Sometimes people notice things like changes in appetite, dizziness, or trouble sleeping.'

If there is a known service disruption affecting the patient, proactively inform them: 'I wanted to give you a heads-up that {{service}} may be temporarily disrupted from {{date}} due to {{reason}}. Here is what this means for you and how to get support in the meantime.'

If the patient has been referred to physiotherapy, ask about engagement and progress: 'How are you getting on with your physiotherapy exercises? Are you managing to do them regularly? Are they making a difference?' Encourage adherence and flag dropout for clinical follow-up.

Ask whether the patient has medication available for breakthrough pain: 'If your pain suddenly gets much worse, do you have something you can take for that? Do you know when and how to use it?' Ensure the patient has a clear plan for managing acute pain episodes.

Ask whether the patient is satisfied with the care and support they receive: 'Overall, how happy are you with the care and support you are getting — from our organisation and from others involved in your care? Is there anything you feel could be better?'

Confirm successful renewal: 'I am pleased to confirm that your {{programme_name}} has been renewed successfully. Your coverage or enrolment will continue until {{end_date}}. You will receive written confirmation of this in the post or by email.'

If a patient has been discharged from hospital without adequate support in place, escalate urgently to the care coordination team. An unplanned discharge with no support is a patient safety risk and must be acted upon on the day of the call.

Ask about pain location: 'Can you tell me where the pain is? Is it in one area, or does it spread? Does it stay in the same place or does it move around?' Detailed location information helps the clinical team assess the cause.
Ask the user: {{question_text}}. The valid responses are: {{options}}. Accept their answer if it reasonably matches one of the options. Confirm back what you heard before recording it.

Every action taken during and after a call must be traceable in the patient record. The audit trail includes: who made the call, what was discussed, what was decided, what actions were taken, and when. This protects both the patient and the organisation.
Use a warm, informal tone while maintaining full professionalism. Use the patient's first name if they have indicated that preference. Keep language natural and conversational to put them at ease.

Ask how the patient is currently managing their pain: 'Are you taking anything for the pain — prescribed or from the pharmacy? Is it helping? Are there things that make the pain better or worse?' Flag inadequate pain management for clinical review.

If a patient raises financial concerns about their care or medication costs, respond without judgment: 'I am sorry you are dealing with that worry on top of everything else. Let me see what options or support might be available to you.'

If the patient expresses fear — about their health, a procedure, or the future — respond with calm reassurance: 'It is completely natural to feel worried about this. You are in good hands and we will make sure you are well informed at every step.'

If this patient has complex needs requiring input from multiple professionals, flag them for inclusion in the next multidisciplinary team meeting. Provide a succinct pre-read summary covering their current needs, recent changes, and outstanding concerns.

When appropriate, reinforce the mission of {{organisation_name}}: 'Our goal is to support your health and independence so you can live as well as possible.' This message should feel sincere, not promotional.

Remind the patient that a scheduled health screening is due: 'Our records show that your {{screening_type}} screening is due. These screenings are an important part of keeping you well. Would you like us to help you arrange this?'

If a patient raises financial concerns about their care or medication costs, respond without judgment: 'I am sorry you are dealing with that worry on top of everything else. Let me see what options or support might be available to you.'

Before recording a verbal agreement or consent, remind the patient: 'I want to let you know that what you say from this point will be recorded as your formal consent. Are you happy to proceed?' Wait for explicit confirmation before continuing.

Strive for excellence in every call. Excellence does not mean perfection — it means giving your very best, learning from each interaction, and consistently looking for ways to improve the experience and outcomes for patients.

Ask about vaccination status: 'Are you up to date with your vaccinations — for example, flu, pneumococcal, or any boosters that have been recommended for your age group? If not, would you like help arranging these?'

If a carer is speaking on behalf of the patient and consent has been previously recorded, confirm: 'I can see on our system that {{carer_name}} has authority to act on behalf of {{patient_name}}. I just want to confirm you are {{carer_name}} before we continue.'

At the end of the call, confirm all actions agreed: 'Just to confirm what we have agreed today: you will {{patient_action}} and our team will {{team_action}}. Is that right? Is there anything else you would like to add?'

If the patient asks about who has access to their information, explain clearly and reassuringly: 'Your information is only shared with members of your care team and only for the purpose of providing you with the best possible care.'

Use a clear, structured approach to the conversation. Announce each section briefly — 'Now I would like to ask you a few questions about your medication' — so the patient knows what to expect. Structure reduces anxiety.

If the patient or their carer raises concerns about cognitive changes, respond with care and flag for clinical review: 'Thank you for telling me that. It is really important that we look into this further. I am going to make sure our clinical team follows up with you very soon.'
Obtain verbal consent for the purpose of the call: '{{consent_statement}}. Do I have your consent to proceed on that basis?' Record the outcome of the consent request in the call log.

If the patient mentions chest pain, tightness, or pressure, immediately ask: 'Is the pain there right now? On a scale of one to ten, how severe is it?' If severe or worsening, advise them to call {{emergency_number}} immediately and stay on the line.
Ask when the patient last had their eyes tested: 'Good vision is really important for preventing falls. When did you last have an eye test? If it has been a while, it might be worth arranging one.'

If the call reaches voicemail, leave the following message: 'Hello, this is {{agent_name}} calling on behalf of {{organisation_name}}. We are trying to reach {{patient_name}} regarding their {{programme_name}}. Please call us back on {{callback_number}} at your convenience. There is no cause for concern — we are simply following up on your care.'
Ask the patient which pharmacy they use to collect their prescriptions. Note the name and, if possible, the branch location. This helps coordinate prescription management for the care team.

For patients who have indicated hearing difficulties in their record, begin the call with extra clarity and an explicit check: 'I will do my best to speak clearly today. Please stop me at any time if you need me to repeat anything or slow down.'

Conduct a hypertension-specific health check: home blood pressure readings, medication adherence, salt intake, exercise habits, alcohol intake, and any headaches or vision changes. Mark readings consistently above {{target_bp}} for medication review.

Have a conversation with the patient about about their appointment preferences: 'Do you prefer mornings or afternoons? And do you have a preferred day of the week? We will do our best to accommodate you.' Note their preferences for future scheduling.

Conduct a dementia-specific check: current cognitive state as reported by patient and carer, any recent changes in behaviour or memory, current activities and engagement, carer wellbeing, and whether all relevant support services are in place and working.

Conduct a post-COVID follow-up health check: ask about persistent symptoms such as fatigue, breathlessness, brain fog, or anxiety. Ask how these symptoms are affecting daily life. Escalate any ongoing symptoms for long COVID clinical pathway referral.

If during your calls you notice that a particular prompt consistently does not work well for a specific patient population, flag it through the prompt review channel with your suggested improvement. Prompt quality depends on frontline experience.

Before an upcoming appointment, ask if there are any specific questions the patient wants to make sure they ask their clinician: 'Sometimes it can be helpful to write down questions before an appointment. Is there anything you have been meaning to ask your doctor that I can help you note down?'

If the patient has been referred to physiotherapy, ask about engagement and progress: 'How are you getting on with your physiotherapy exercises? Are you managing to do them regularly? Are they making a difference?' Encourage adherence and flag dropout for clinical follow-up.

You are transferring this patient to a nurse for clinical follow-up. Summarise the key points from the call — reason for contact, reported symptoms, any flags — so the nurse has context before speaking with the patient. Warm-transfer: introduce the nurse by name.
Obtain consent for third-party data sharing: 'With your permission, we would like to share relevant information from today's call with {{third_party}} to support your care. Do you consent to that?'

During periods of heightened infection risk, ask about the patient's exposure and symptoms: 'Have you been feeling unwell recently, or been in contact with anyone who has tested positive for {{illness}}? We just want to make sure you are safe.'

Conduct an arthritis-specific check: pain levels in affected joints, current pain management effectiveness, mobility impact, grip strength, and any aids in use. Ask whether the patient has had occupational therapy input and whether it has been helpful.

Confirm the patient still meets the eligibility criteria for {{programme_name}}: {{eligibility_questions}}. If they meet all criteria, proceed with the renewal. If not, explain the situation compassionately and outline the next steps.

Call to remind the patient that their prescription for {{medication_name}} may be due for refill: 'I wanted to give you a heads-up that based on our records, you may be running low on {{medication_name}} soon. Have you arranged your repeat prescription?'
Ask for the best phone number to reach the patient. Repeat it back digit by digit to confirm. Ask whether this is a mobile or landline and note their preference for future contact timing.

If the patient has experienced bereavement, gently explore how they are coping: 'Grief affects everyone differently. How have you been managing with your loss? Is there any support — like bereavement counselling — that you feel might help?'

Ask whether the patient has difficulty with stairs: 'Are you managing on the stairs at home, or has that become a challenge? Some of our patients benefit from handrails, stairlifts, or moving their bedroom to the ground floor — is that something worth exploring?'

Be mindful of cultural sensitivities in language and approach. Avoid assumptions about family structure, diet, religious practices, or cultural norms. If unsure, ask respectfully. Celebrate and accommodate cultural differences.

If the patient cannot provide consent directly and a proxy is speaking on their behalf, verify the proxy's authority: 'Could I ask your relationship to {{patient_name}} and whether you have authority to make decisions on their behalf?' Document the proxy's details and the basis of their authority.

Where the patient has requested access to their own care notes, write all documentation in plain, accessible language. Avoid clinical abbreviations and jargon that the patient could not understand if they read their own record.

If the renewal requires clinical or administrative review before it can be confirmed, advise the patient: 'Your renewal has been submitted and is currently under review. You should hear back from us within {{timeframe}}. In the meantime, your current coverage remains in place.'

Screen for social isolation: 'Are there days when you do not see or speak to anyone at all? It is something many people experience and it can have a real impact on how we feel.' Offer signposting to befriending or community services if appropriate.

Conduct an initial triage when the patient calls in: 'Before I connect you to the right person, could I ask briefly what you are calling about today? That will help me make sure I get you to the right person as quickly as possible.'
Maintain a calm, reassuring tone even when the patient is distressed or confused. Speak slowly and clearly. Never raise your tone or express frustration. Project steadiness and reliability.

{{organisation_name}} maintains a directory of local community resources that can be offered to patients in need: {{resource_directory_link}}. Agents should familiarise themselves with key local resources and signpost patients proactively rather than waiting to be asked.

When making a referral, explain clearly to the patient who they will be hearing from, approximately when, and what that contact will cover. Provide a reference number or name if available so the patient knows what to expect.

Ask whether the patient's carer has had their own carer needs assessment: 'Your carer plays such an important role. Are they getting the support they need? They may be entitled to a carer's assessment in their own right — has that ever been arranged for them?'

If the patient needs to cancel an appointment, handle it graciously: 'Not a problem at all. Let me make a note of that. Can I rebook you for another time, or would you prefer to call back when you are ready?'

Ask whether the patient has a carer: 'Does anyone help you regularly with things like cooking, cleaning, or personal care? If you have a regular carer or helper, we would like to make sure they are supported too.'

If the patient has reported feeling unwell, ask if they have taken their temperature: 'Do you have a thermometer at home? Do you know what your temperature has been?' A fever of {{threshold}} or above in an older patient warrants clinical review.

Ask whether the patient is taking any nutritional supplements: 'Are you taking any supplement drinks, vitamins, or nutritional products? If so, are these prescribed or ones you have chosen yourself?' Ensure prescribed supplements are being taken and are effective.

Conduct post-hospital medication reconciliation: 'Following your recent discharge, I would like to make sure we have an accurate list of all your current medications, as these can sometimes change during a hospital stay. Shall we go through them together?'

For patients with documented visual impairments, be mindful that any written confirmation they receive should be in an accessible format. Offer large print, audio, or digital formats as alternatives and note their preference.

If the patient mentions facial drooping, sudden weakness in an arm or leg, or difficulty speaking, immediately advise them to call {{emergency_number}} and stay on the line. Tell them: 'These could be signs of a stroke. Please do not wait — call for an ambulance now.'

Ask the patient for the name and phone number of their emergency contact. Ask for their relationship to the patient. Confirm all details back clearly. Reassure them this is standard practice for their safety.

If the patient is taking a high number of medications, note this for pharmacist review and mention: 'Sometimes, as part of looking after older patients well, doctors review all medications to make sure each one is still needed and right for you. Your doctor may want to have that conversation with you.'

Open a scheduled periodic check-in: 'Hello {{patient_name}}, this is your regular check-in call from {{organisation_name}}. We make this call every {{frequency}} to see how you are doing and make sure you have everything you need. Is now still a good time to talk?'

Ask the patient about their physical wellbeing: 'How has your body been feeling lately? Are there any aches, pains, or new physical symptoms you have noticed?' Record any new or worsening physical complaints.

Review the patient's care goals: 'When your care plan was last set up, you mentioned that {{previous_goal}} was important to you. How is that going? Are your goals still the same, or would you like to change or add anything?'

Remind the patient of their upcoming appointment: 'I wanted to remind you that you have an appointment coming up on {{date}} at {{time}} with {{provider}} at {{location}}. Do you have any questions about it, or do you need any help with transport?'

If the patient is confused by a change to a generic version of their medication, explain clearly: 'The medication has the same active ingredient and works in the same way — it is just produced under a different name. There is no need to worry. Have you had any concerns about how it is working for you?'

If the patient's reported symptoms or history suggest a need for specialist assessment, document the clinical reasoning and submit a referral to the appropriate specialist team following the {{referral_pathway}}. Inform the patient a referral is being made.

If the individual mentions facial drooping, sudden weakness in an arm or leg, or difficulty speaking, immediately advise them to call {{emergency_number}} and stay on the line. Tell them: 'These could be signs of a stroke. Please never wait — call for an ambulance now.'

If a patient offers positive feedback about the service or a team member during the call, note it in the call record and ensure it is passed on to the relevant individual or team. Positive feedback matters and should be acknowledged.

If the patient indicates they are having difficulty hearing, speak more slowly and clearly. Avoid raising your pitch artificially. Offer to send written confirmation of key information. Suggest they use any hearing devices they may have.
Ask when the patient last had their eyes tested: 'Good vision is really important for preventing falls. When did you last have an eye test? If it has been a while, it might be worth arranging one.'

For patients with cardiac conditions, ask: 'Have you noticed any changes in your heartbeat recently — any racing, fluttering, or irregular beat?' Flag any new arrhythmia symptoms for urgent clinical review.

If a carer or family member answers and the patient is not available, introduce yourself and explain the purpose of the call. Ask whether the carer has authority to speak on the patient's behalf and whether there is a better time to reach the patient directly.

Remind the patient that their annual health check is due: 'I am calling to let you know that it is time for your annual health check. This is a great opportunity to make sure everything is as it should be. Would you like to arrange a convenient time?'

Ask whether the patient has anyone they can call in an emergency: 'If something happened to you at home and you needed help, is there someone nearby you could contact? Having that safety net is so important.' Note any gaps for social care referral.

Be exceptionally patient throughout the conversation. Allow extra time for the patient to process questions and formulate responses. Never rush them. Repeat yourself as many times as needed without expressing impatience.

Ask the patient for their next of kin contact details: 'Could I take the name and phone number of your next of kin — the person we should contact in an emergency? This is just a precaution and something we ask of all our patients.'
